articles.title: Woman's Part
articles.author: Vernessa Miller Nagle
articles.text:
FROM the slave of man to man's equal socially, politically, and intellectually, represents many years of growth on the part of woman. Time was when women, theoretically, were perpetual minors, subjected to the control of fathers, husbands, or guardians. Through the years woman has made strides in feminine progress.

From Eden to Christ represents chronologically a long period of human growth. Out of this dim past emerges annals of the achievements of such characters as Rebekah, mother of Jacob; Miriam, the prophetess; Rachael, the beloved; Helen of Troy; Ruth, the gleaner; Abigail, the beautiful peacemaker; Dido, founder of Carthage; Sappho, greatest Greek poetess; and numerous other personalities.

During the Medieval Ages woman began gradually to outgrow her traditional bondage. We recall the name of Philippa, founder of Queen's College, England; Beatrice, inspiration of Dante; Laura, immortalized by Petrarch; Joan of Arc; and Anne of Bretagne, patroness of learning.

Among those who achieved renown and accomplished deeds during the nineteenth century are Lucretia Mott, Mary Lyon, Frances E. Willard, Clara Barton, Florence Nightingale, and others far too numerous to mention.

Of the achievements of woman during the twentieth century we are more or less familiar. Woman has taken her place beside man in the professions, industries, politics, art, and science. At the time the Nineteenth Amendment became a part of our law there was ended a struggle of almost a century's duration. At this time suffrage had gained acceptance in many countries of the world.

As civilization has progressed through the ages, mankind has learned that the most desirable society is the result of men and women working side by side, shouldering alike the responsibilities of our nation's betterment and growth.

AND now, after a struggle that has extended over a period of many hundred years, has woman become more or less passive? Has she become self-satisfied with the assurance that her laurels have been won, and that there are no new fields of endeavor? Is it possible that we are resting on our oars? It is not so much the need of new fields of conquest that should concern women as the giving of her talent to the problems that encompass our every-day life.

Never in the history of our nation has there been greater need for women to strive for the solidarity of our people. We must set up the kind of homes that will build for the security of tomorrow's society. Let us rediscover the loyalty of our pioneer mothers. Let us exercise the foresight of enlightened women of today.

Among the nations of the world there are constant threats of war. The rattling of sabres is heard in the distance. Have we American women sufficiently united our efforts and thoughts on peace?
